title: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.2 — Operational risk-assessment: per-change risk-score recalculation
exclusions: Does NOT replace the architect's design review; detects only that a material change (asset scope, data classification, exposure) occurred without a corresponding risk-score recalculation within 7 days.
evidence: Saved search 'iso_27001_8_2_change_risk_recalc' (daily) outputs per change: change_id, asset, old_score, new_score, delta, recalc_performed, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Clause 8.2 Information security risk assessment at planned intervals or on significant change — directly evidenced.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A closed change whose linked risk_id shows no change in score within 7d sets recalc_performed=0 and appears in the report.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A change followed by a score update within 7d is absent.

dataSources: `index=change` sourcetype=servicenow:change; `index=grc` sourcetype=archer:risk_score; change-to-risk mapping `iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv`. (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
app: Splunk Enterprise Security, Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow
spl:
index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change state=closed earliest=-14d
| rename affected_ci AS asset
| lookup iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv asset OUTPUT risk_id
| join type=outer risk_id
  [search index=grc sourcetype=archer:risk_score earliest=-14d
   | stats latest(score) AS new_score earliest(score) AS old_score latest(_time) AS recalc_time BY risk_id]
| eval delta=new_score-old_score
| eval recalc_performed=if(recalc_time>=_time AND recalc_time<=_time+604800,1,0)
| where recalc_performed=0 AND abs(delta)>0
| table change_id asset risk_id old_score new_score delta recalc_performed
description: Joins closed changes against their linked risk-register entries and asserts that a score recalculation was produced within 7 days. Clause 8.2 requires the organisation to perform information-security risk assessments 'at planned intervals or when significant changes are proposed' — this UC is the 'significant change' trigger.
value: Gives Clause 8.2 an operational verification signal instead of an annual narrative. Audit objection 'where is the evidence the risk was reassessed after the 2024-Q3 PCI change?' becomes a one-query answer.
implementation: (1) Link every CMDB change to affected risks in iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv; (2) normalise Archer risk_score events; (3) schedule UC daily; (4) recalc_performed=0 opens an ISMS action to the risk owner; (5) monthly report to risk committee. Indexes required: `index=change`, `index=grc`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=archer:risk_score`, `sourcetype=archer:risk_score;`, `sourcetype=servicenow:change`, `sourcetype=servicenow:change;`.
visualization: Scatter of change impact vs recalc latency, table of changes missing recalc, single value 'changes in 14d missing recalc'. Key fields: `asset`, `change_id`, `delta`, `new_score`, `old_score`, `recalc_performed`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Assessment cycle alignment** — Control assessments following annual cycles may show brief non-current periods between assessment completion and report publication.

2. **Finding remediation within SLA** — Open findings within their documented remediation timeline are being addressed per policy, not neglected or overlooked.

3. **Framework version transitions** — Migration between control framework versions (ISO 27001:2013 to 2022) creates temporary mapping gaps during the documented transition period.

4. **Accepted residual risk items** — Findings formally accepted through the risk management governance process with documented compensating controls and management sign-off.

5. **Evidence collection cadence differences** — Controls with different evidence generation frequencies (real-time vs. quarterly) show varying currency in compliance dashboards.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `change`, `grc` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `8.2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 8.2 (Information-security risk assessment) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.48: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.2 — Operational risk-assessment: per-change risk-score recalculation." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `change`, `grc` under `servicenow:change`, `archer:risk_score`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Link every CMDB change to affected risks in iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** normalise Archer risk_score events. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC daily. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** recalc_performed=0 opens an ISMS action to the risk owner. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** monthly report to risk committee. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.2 — Operational risk-assessment: per-change risk-score recalculation," the aggregation exposes change activity — change frequency, change authorship, and change surface are the dimensions the regulation cares about, not raw events.

This search correlates `index=change` (sourcetype=`servicenow:change`) on the outer side with `index=grc` (sourcetype=`archer:risk_score`) on the inner side, joined on `risk_id` across the last 14 days. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.48: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.2 — Operational risk-assessment:]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -14d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.48,reg=ISO/IEC_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `change_id`, `affected_ci`, `state`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table change_id, affected_ci, state, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup iso_27001_change_to_risk.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.48: ISO/IEC 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Scatter Of Change Impact Vs Recalc Latency (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Changes Missing Recalc (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Changes In 14D Missing Recalc' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO/IEC 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.2 — Operational risk-assessment: per-change risk-score recalculation. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
